coredata.dc:title: Ultra-fast microwave-assisted synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with an ultra-high quantum yield for H2O2 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoyu
Zhang, Mengyuan
Zhang, Zhenghua
coredata.dc:description: The conventional hydrothermal bottom-up synthesis for preparing carbon dots (CDs) usually requires stringent conditions and high energy consumption, whereas the quantum yield (QY) of CDs remains at a relatively low level. Herein, CDs exhibiting strong blue photoluminescence (PL) with an ultra-high QY (up to 94.4%) were prepared via a simple one-step microwave-assisted route using citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as precursors. The operating conditions, including reaction time, solvent species, amount of solvent, and precursor molar ratio, were optimized. The CDs synthesized within 3.5 min of microwave heating in ethylene glycol (EG) at a CA-to-PD molar ratio of 5:1 exhibited the highest QY (94.4%). A synergistic PL mechanism of the molecule state and carbon core state emissive centers was proposed according to the characterization results of CDs. Moreover, the proof-to-concept H2O2 fluorescent detection performances of the prepared CDs confirmed their great application potential as probe materials. Through directly mixing the CD products and H2O2, a strong PL response could be rapidly detected after merely 1 min, and the detection limit was calculated to be as low as 8.6 nM. Besides, the CDs exhibited high biocompatibility, making them suitable for in-vivo detection of H2O2. Therefore, this work provides a novel and efficient approach for synthesis of ultra-high QY CDs that can be directly applied in biological detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Nanomaterials
Microwave treatment
H2O2 detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Committee of Science and Technology Innovation of Shenzhen
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52170041 ), Tsinghua SIGS Start-up Funding ( QD2020002N ), and the Committee of Science and Technology Innovation of Shenzhen ( JCYJ20190813163401660 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the preparation process of CDs.

(a) QY of CDs prepared using different carbon and nitrogen sources in various solvents. (b) QY of CDs prepared using CA and phenylenediamine with different molecular configurations in EG. The reaction conditions: all the precursor solutions were prepared with a fixed molar ratio of carbon to nitrogen sources (1:0.2) in 10 mL solvents, and treated in a domestic microwave oven at 490 W for 3.5 min.
QY of CDs prepared under different reaction conditions: (a) time, (b) amount of EG, and (c) molar ratio of CA to o-PD. (d) A comparison of QY values and processing time with previous reports.
(a) TEM images of CDs. (b) XRD pattern of CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (d) Survey XPS spectrum of CDs. (e) C 1 s, (f) O 1 s, and (g) N 1 s XPS spectra.

(a) UV-Vis spectrum and PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (b) Second MS spectrum of the parent ion with m/z = 229. The inset shows the formation of the DBCA molecule. (c) Formation of possible fluorescent molecules based on the MS results of ion fragments.
Schematic diagram of CD synthesis in the one-step microwave-assisted procedure.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs at (a) short and (b) long excitation wavelength bands. (c) Variation of emission color at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Excitation-dependent behaviors of CDs. (e) Experimental and theoretical PL decay curve of CDs.
(a) Linear calibration plot of the F0/F parameter as a function of H2O2 concentration. (b) CD detection performance in this study compared to previous reports.

head.ce:title: Ultra-fast microwave-assisted synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with an ultra-high quantum yield for H2O2 detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Engineering & Nano-Technology, Tsinghua Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conventional hydrothermal bottom-up synthesis for preparing carbon dots (CDs) usually requires stringent conditions and high energy consumption, whereas the quantum yield (QY) of CDs remains at a relatively low level. Herein, CDs exhibiting strong blue photoluminescence (PL) with an ultra-high QY (up to 94.4%) were prepared via a simple one-step microwave-assisted route using citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as precursors. The operating conditions, including reaction time, solvent species, amount of solvent, and precursor molar ratio, were optimized. The CDs synthesized within 3.5 min of microwave heating in ethylene glycol (EG) at a CA-to-PD molar ratio of 5:1 exhibited the highest QY (94.4%). A synergistic PL mechanism of the molecule state and carbon core state emissive centers was proposed according to the characterization results of CDs. Moreover, the proof-to-concept H2O2 fluorescent detection performances of the prepared CDs confirmed their great application potential as probe materials. Through directly mixing the CD products and H2O2, a strong PL response could be rapidly detected after merely 1 min, and the detection limit was calculated to be as low as 8.6 nM. Besides, the CDs exhibited high biocompatibility, making them suitable for in-vivo detection of H2O2. Therefore, this work provides a novel and efficient approach for synthesis of ultra-high QY CDs that can be directly applied in biological detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery in 2004 [1], carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have been considered a superior alternative to traditional fluorescent dyes and quantum dots. In addition to their excellent photoluminescent (PL) properties, CDs exhibit low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and high water solubility [2,3]. Therefore, CDs have great potential in diverse application fields such as photocatalysis [4,5], biological fluorescent imaging [6–8], light-emitting diodes [9,10], chemical sensors [11–13], drug delivery [14,15] and fluorescent analysis [16–18]. Recent investigations have been mainly focusing on the development of efficient, reproducible, and controllable synthesis routes to fabricate CDs with excellent performance including high quantum yields (QY), red or near-infrared (NIR) emission, multi-color emission, and room-temperature phosphorescence [19–21].

The modern CD synthesis techniques can be classified into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [22]. The top-down approaches utilize macroscopic carbon materials to form nanosized structures through ablation or oxidation [23–25], while the bottom-up ways employ molecular precursors to fabricate CDs via polycondensation and carbonization [26]. Compared to the top-down methods, the bottom-up approaches have the advantages of a broad variety of precursors [27], relatively low cost, and simple operation [22]. Citric acid (CA) is the most common carbon precursor to fabricate CDs via the bottom-up synthesis due to its nontoxicity and widespread availability [28]. While CA can serve as the carbon source for building carbon backbones in CDs, nitrogen-containing precursors have been shown to enhance the QY of CDs via doping in the carbon core structure or by forming molecular fluorophores [26].

Hydrothermal treatment is the most adopted bottom-up method to prepare CDs because of its relatively facile operating procedure, in which a precursor solution is sealed and heated to the reaction temperature [22,29]. However, hydrothermal methods commonly require high temperatures over 200 °C, high pressures of about 30 atm, and long reaction times of about 3–24 h. Thus, hydrothermal methods are quite energy-inefficient and time-consuming [16,30,31]. An alternative approach for the convenient CD synthesis is the microwave-assisted bottom-up route which can effectively reduce the reaction time and provide a more uniform CD size distribution without the use of harmful solvents [16,22,32,33]. However, CDs prepared via microwave-assisted methods generally possess low QY values, mostly below 50%, which limits their application range to a large extent [16,33–35]. Notably, Zhang et al. [36] fabricated CDs with QY values higher than 90% via microwave-assisted synthesis. However, their fabrication process required a laborious temperature controlled operation, including at least 8 different heating periods and several intervals to avoid solution bumping. In addition, frequently repeated heating and cooling microwave steps negatively impact the operational lifetime of microwave ovens and increase operational risks. Therefore, developing a simplified synthesis method for producing CDs with high QY is an urgent task.

Because of their low toxicity and water-solubility, CDs have been considered as appropriate probe materials in fluorescence spectroscopy, which relies on the variation in fluorescence intensity with changing concentration of a target chemical [37]. Compared to the conventional detection methods, fluorescence analysis features high sensitivity, rapid response, simple operation and great portability [38,39]. However, the common fluorescent materials like metal quantum dots, organic fluorescent dyes, and semiconductor nanomaterials are quite toxic and water-insoluble [17,37]. Besides, these generally require advanced skills in synthesis and thorough purification stage [17], which extremely limits their practical application. H2O2 is known as one of the most important reactive oxygen species playing a significant role in biological activities such as enzymatic reactions and signal transduction [40–42]. Despite the fact that fluorescent materials have already been widely utilized in H2O2 detection, certain gaps remain to be further fulfilled. Specifically, numerous fluorescent probes lack high water solubility or great biocompatibility, which makes them unsuitable for biological detection [43,44]. Moreover, the fast response to H2O2 should be emphasized due to the possible interruption from metabolic reactions in living organisms [18,45], whereas most of materials exhibit the prolonged response time (over 10 min). As for CDs, researchers have revealed their relatively high detection limit or low sensitivity to H2O2 [16,40]. In order to enhance the sensitivity of CDs to H2O2, these require the use of the stoichiometric relationship in reactions with certain metal ions, which makes the process more laborious [17,46].

Herein, an extremely simple one-step microwave-assisted procedure was proposed to synthesize a series of CDs in a short period of time. The precursor species and operating conditions (reaction time, solvent species, amount of solvent, and molar ratio of the precursors) were selected and optimized. The CDs prepared using CA and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) dissolved in ethylene glycol (EG) solvent exhibited the highest QY of up to 94.4% within a merely 3.5 min. The fabricated CDs were then systematically characterized in terms of their morphology, composition, and fluorescence performances. Specifically, these synthesized CDs needed no extra purification and could be directly utilized in the H2O2 fluorescence analysis. An instant and quite rapid response toward H2O2 was observed with a calculated detection limit of 8.6 nM and a response time of 1 min. Finally, due to great water solubility, non-cytotoxicity, and fine enough particle size (< 5 nm), the CDs produced in this study are the promising candidates as the fluorescent probe in biological H2O2 detection.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade. 1,2-Diaminobenzene (C6H8N2), 1,5-naphthalenediamine (C10H10N2), polyethylene glycol (PEG-200, HO(CH2CH2O)nH, average MW = 200), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (C10H10N2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. EG (C2H6O2). Methyl alcohol (CH6O2) and ethyl alcohol (C2H6O) were supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. CA (C6H8O7) and L(+)-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) were produced by Shandong Xiya Chemical Reagent Factory. H2O2 solution (GR, 30 wt% in H2O) was provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

Different carbon and nitrogen sources, including CA, ascorbic acid, ethylenediamine, 1,2-diaminobenzene, 1,5-diaminonaphthalene, and o-PD, were selected as precursors. Methanol, ethanol, water, EG, and PEG were utilized as solvents. The effects of reaction time (1–10 min), amount of solvent (5–30 mL), and the molar ratio of the carbon to nitrogen sources (1:5–1:0.05) on QY were also evaluated and compared. The details concerning the synthesis of CDs are displayed in Fig. 1. First, precursors were mixed with varying carbon-to-nitrogen source molar ratios in the solvent. Then, the mixtures were treated in a domestic microwave oven at 490 W for different reaction times, and could subsequently be utilized as fluorescent probe materials. The highest QY CDs were fabricated as follows: 1) 0.961 g (5 mmol) of CA and 0.1081 g (1 mmol) of o-PD were directly added into 10 mL of EG without extra treatment (such as stirring or sonification); 2) the blend was loaded in a household microwave oven and treated at 490 W for 3.5 min. The CD products were viscous brown solutions without visible solid particles, which were afterward used for characterization and application.

The photoluminescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 300 W Xe lamp as the light source. The UV-Vis absorption spectra were acquired by a spectrophotometer (UV-3010, Hitachi, Japan). The size and morphology of the CDs were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100 F, JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher, USA), Raman spectrometer (iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA) with 1064 nm as the excitation wavelength, X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI-5000, Versaprobe II, Japan) were applied to analyze the chemical composition of the CDs. The physical composition of the CDs was studied through the mass-charge ratio measurements performed on a coupled liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) instrument (1290UPLC-QTOF6550, Agilent, USA), and the primary mass spectra of the CD samples as well as the secondary mass spectra of targeted parent ions were acquired. The details on the LC-MS operation parameters are provided in Table S1. The fluorescence lifetime measurements are described in Text S1.

Quinine sulfate, with a 54% absolute QY, was adopted as a standard to determine the relative QY of the CDs. The QY of the CDs was estimated by comparing the ratio of their integrated PL intensities (excited at 350 nm) to their UV absorbance values (at 350 nm). The UV absorbance values of the CDs and quinine sulfate solutions were both adjusted to 0.05 to guarantee equal absorption strength. The QY values were calculated according to the following equation [33]:
(1)
QY
x
=
QY
st
∙
I
x
/
I
st
∙
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
∙
A
st
/
A
x
where “st” and “x” represent the standard quinine sulfate and CDs, respectively; I is the integrated fluorescence; η is the solvent refractive index. Here, the value of η
x /η
st is 1 when EG and pure water are used as solvents. A is the UV absorbance, and the A
st /A
x ratio is equal to 1 because of the equivalent absorption strengths.

Certain dosages of fabricated CD solutions were firstly dispersed in 10 mL water. The PL intensity of this aqueous solution was then measured and recorded as F0. After that 100 μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations were added into the mixture samples, of which the PL intensity F was measured after 1 min at room temperature. Both the F0 and F values were found at the emission wavelength of 424 nm. The change in fluorescence strength is denoted as ΔF (ΔF = F0-F), whereas the ΔF/F0 ratio is adopted as the measuring parameter and plotted against the H2O2 concentration to acquire the calibration curve for H2O2 detection via fluorescence analysis. In a practical H2O2 concentration detection process, 100 μL of H2O2 sample is added into the prepared CD suspension, and the PL intensity of this mixture is recorded 1 min after. Based on obtained calibration curve, corresponding H2O2 concentration can be calculated accordingly.

Fig. 2 displays the effects of different carbon and nitrogen sources as well as solvents on the QY of the CDs. According to Fig. 2a, the highest QY of up to 94.4% was achieved in the CDs synthesized with CA and o-PD in EG. Moreover, two different phenylenediamine isomers, m-PD and p-PD, were also applied as nitrogen sources for comparison with o-PD. Their corresponding CD products exhibited the QY values as low as 29.14% and 4.94%, respectively (Fig. 2b). This result matches the conclusion reported in a previous study, in which CA-based CDs were endowed with a higher QY when prepared with a,b-diamines as nitrogen sources compared with diamines in other configurations [47].

After determining the optimal precursors and solvents, the effects of reaction time, solvent volume, and precursor molar ratio on the QY of the CDs were further investigated to optimize the CD synthesis conditions. The reaction time determined the final reaction degree of the precursors. The color of the obtained CD solutions became darker as the reaction time was extended, which was due to the enriched content of the carbon cores with a higher carbonization degree. According to Fig. 3a, the highest QY was achieved for the CDs after 3.5 min of microwave treatment. Then, the precursor concentration was adjusted by altering the solvent volume. Fixed amounts of CA (5 mmol) and o-PD (1 mmol) were added to different volumes of EG for 3.5-min microwave treatment. As shown in Fig. 3b, the CDs prepared using CA (5 mmol) and o-PD (1 mmol) dissolved in 10 mL EG demonstrated the highest QY. The presence of EG was predicted to affect the probability of intermolecular collisions, thus influencing the reaction rate of the precursors and the proportion of fluorescent molecules, polymer chains, and carbogenic cores in the final product [48,49]. The molar ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources also had a significant effect on the QY of the CD products. As seen in Fig. 3c, the highest QY was attained at a molar ratio of 1:0.2. This can be deducted that the different molar ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources will affect the final content of molecular fluorophores and carbon network structures in the final CD products, which can lead to the varying QY since the effective proportion of PL centers changes accordingly. Moreover, the color of the prepared CD solutions gradually became darker as this ratio was increased. It is known that CA serves as the main contributor to carbon backbones in CDs [26]. Therefore, the darker color of these CD products can be ascribed to the increase in the carbon core content on account of abundant CA molecules.

Ultimately, 10 mL EG and a molar ratio of 1:0.2 (CA to o-PD) during the 3.5-min microwave treatment were identified as the optimized conditions for the fabrication of CDs with the highest QY (up to 94.4%). A comparative analysis with CDs synthesized via different methods in aspects of processing time and product QY is displayed in Fig. 3d, revealing the simplicity of the adopted synthetic route and excellent fluorescent properties of the prepared CDs. These optimized CDs were selected for further characterization and application.

Fig. 4a depicts the TEM images, revealing the uniformly dispersed CDs with no aggregation. These CDs exhibited a narrow size distribution with the average diameter of 3.5 nm. Meanwhile, a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm corresponded to the (102) facets in the graphitic structure [50]. The XRD pattern of the CDs shows a single peak centered at 2θ = 21.5° (Fig. 4b), which can be attributed to the (002) planes of disordered sp3 carbon [51]. Based on the low graphitization degree of the carbon core network and the apparently amorphous structure, the fabricated CDs were therefore classified as carbon polymer dots (CPDs) [49].

To further interpret the structure of the CDs, their chemical composition was also investigated. In particular, the FTIR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the functional groups in the CDs. Fig. 4c displays the FTIR spectrum, in which a strong broad absorption peak at 3340 cm−1 was referred to the high content of hydroxyl and amino groups [52]. The peak at 1640 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bonds. The peaks at 1460 and 1336 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC and C–N bonds, respectively. The features at 1208, 1084, and 1044 cm−1 demonstrated the existence of ester bonds and C–O–C/C–O groups. Moreover, the bands at 672 and 880 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibrations of C–H groups and benzene rings, which indicated a typical carbon conjugated structure [30,53]. Besides, the FTIR spectra of the CDs synthesized in water and EG were compared in Fig. S1 to gain a further understanding of the effect of solvent. Unlike the CDs synthesized in water (green line), those produced in EG (red line) exhibited the peaks at 1044 and 1084 cm−1 caused by the addition of hydroxyl groups from EG. Moreover, the CDs formed in EG presented an extra peak at 1208 cm−1 which indicated the formation of ester groups. These groups can serve as the internal linkages of carbon networks [30] or as oxygenous defect states, which have been shown to be an origin of blue PL emission [54]. Because water molecules cannot react with CA, no ester groups appeared in the CDs synthesized in water, which was likely the reason why the QY of these CDs was distinctly lower than that of the other CDs. Meanwhile, EG also possesses the stronger reaction activity. Therefore, more ester groups might have formed in these CDs due to the reaction between EG and the CD precursors, which could have resulted in the highest QY. However, the contribution of ester groups to the QY enhancement and the relevant mechanisms are still unclear and require further investigation.

Fig. 4d displays the survey XPS spectrum of the CDs, presenting the C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s peaks at 84, 399, and 532 eV, respectively [55]. In turn, the high-resolution C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s XPS spectra are displayed in Figs. 4e-4g. The C1 spectrum (Fig. 4e) showed three peaks at 284.5, 285.2, and 285.6 eV, which were assigned to sp2 CC, sp3 C–C/C–H, and C–N/C–O species [56,57]. Notably, the features from pyridinic Ns and pyrrolic Ns were identified in the N 1 s spectrum at 398.5 and 400.1 eV [58], suggesting that a nitrogen-doped carbon framework was successfully realized. Besides, it has been shown that pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen structures distributed at the carbon core edges or terminating sites can substantially improve the PL QY of CDs [59,60].

In addition, the Raman spectrum of the CDs synthesized was also demonstrated in Fig. S2. The main scattering bands at 1275, 1355, 1403 and 1461 cm−1 can be attributed to the signals of C-O-C antisymmetric vibration, C-N symmetric vibration, C-C stretching vibration and CH2 scissoring mode, respectively [21]. The Raman result is consistent with FTIR and XPS analyses (Fig. 4).

Investigating the composition of CDs is essential for elucidating the PL mechanism of CDs. Several different species of emissive centers, including molecular state, surface state, and carbon core state PL centers, have been proposed to expound the CD fluorescence mechanism [61,62]. Both the UV-Vis and LC-MS spectra can help shed some light on the composition of the prepared CDs, and thus facilitating the understanding of the CD fluorescence mechanism.

Fig. 5a depicts the fluorescence spectra of the CDs, exhibiting the excitation and emission intensity peaks at 372 and 425 nm, respectively. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs showed strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, which gradually decreased toward the visible region. A distinctive absorption peak at 247 cm−1 was ascribed to the typical aromatic π system absorption caused by the π-π * transitions of the π-conjugated electrons in the sp2 carbon framework [63]. Nevertheless, this sp2 carbon-related peak was blue-shifted relative to the corresponding signal of highly graphitic carbon quantum dots at 280 cm−1, observed in previous research [64]. This blue-shift phenomenon could be explained by the smaller size of sp2 domains in the prepared CDs, which were indicative of more limited crystal lattice formation compared with earlier reported carbon quantum dots [64]. The other two peaks at 268 and 276 cm−1 might be assigned to the π-π * transitions of aromatic carbon bonds or other molecules (e.g. 1-oxo-1 H-pyrido[2,1-b][1,3]benzoxazole-3-carboxylic acid) present in CDs [30,47,65,66]. The broad absorption band at about 384 cm−1 is typically induced by the n-π * transition of functional groups containing nitrogen or oxygen atoms, which are either distributed at the carbon network edge or in molecules. Moreover, the extended band tail in the visible light range could be owing to the absorption from the surface sites with lower energy [63].

The reaction route to form independent fluorescent molecules was first proposed by Song et al. [67] who fabricated CDs using CA and ethylenediamine (EDA) through hydrothermal treatment. These CDs exhibited a high QY of up to 86%, in which a fluorescent molecule, imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylicacid (IPCA), was identified and proven to serve as the primary fluorescent center for bright blue PL emission. Later, Kasprzyk et al. [47] produced several fluorophore-containing structures similar to IPCA based on CA and different amines. These synthesized molecules were also confirmed to play a dominant role in their corresponding CD PL performance. One of these fluorescent molecules, 1-oxo-1,5-dihydropyrido[1,2-a]benzimidazole-3-carboxylic acid (DBCA), was formed from CA and o-PD via hydrothermal synthesis. The precursors used in their study [47] are exactly the same herein and therefore DBCA might be formed (Fig. S3). In order to detect the DBCA molecules in the prepared CDs, a comparative analysis of the UV-Vis absorption spectra, as well as the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, was performed on pure DBCA and CDs. Notably, both kinds of materials well correlated in terms of their optical properties. In particular, the UV-Vis absorption peaks of DBCA at about 220, 250, and 380 nm were all identified in the absorption spectra of the CDs fabricated herein (Fig. 5a and Fig. S3). Moreover, the PL excitation and emission peaks of DBCA at 375 and 425 nm were almost identical to those of the CDs (Fig. 5a and Fig. S3). This comparison demonstrates that DBCA might act as the main fluorescence centers in the CDs under consideration, making a decisive contribution to PL performance of the latter ones.

LC-MS was further adopted to analyze the molecular components in the prepared CDs and confirm the formation of the fluorescent DBCA molecules. A molecule with a mass-to-charge ratio of 229 (DBCA) was identified in several components separated at different retention times (Fig. 5b, Fig. S4 and Table S2). Fig. 5b depicts the second mass spectrum of the fabricated CDs, from which it can be decuded that DBCA could be generated by the reaction between CA and o-PD [47]. The ion fragments of this molecule (Fig. 5b) further demonstrated the existence of DBCA in the prepared CDs (Fig. 5c). As such, the fabricated CDs might have two emissive centers, videlicet molecule state and carbon core state fluorescent centers [68].

Fig. 6 illustrates the schematic of the formation process of CDs in the one-step microwave-treating synthesis route based on the analytical results of this work. Initially, a relative high-temperature environment immediately provided by the microwave leads to the rapid dissolution of the precursors as well as the violent intermolecular collisions. The dehydration and condensation of molecules are extensively initiated at the same time [49]. As the treatment time increases, polymerization occurs, and the monomers are condensed into polymer chains. The abundance of functional groups, revealed by the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4c), makes the polymers crosslinked and entangled, leading to the emergence of numerous polymer clusters. As the reaction proceeds, internal crosslinking within these clusters helps stabilize the carbon core structure. At the same time, the carbonization degree is enhanced, causing to the formation of graphite-like lattice structures. Meanwhile, abundant molecular fluorophores (DBCA) are freely distributed in the solution or attached to the carbon core network via covalent bonds or intermolecular interactions [48,68]. Overall, the combination of fluorophore assembly, polymer crosslinking, and cluster carbonization is conducive to the production of nanosized carbon cores in the CDs (Fig. 6) [67], which demonstrate a high PL QY.

To thoroughly explore the optical properties of the CDs, their PL spectra were obtained at different excitation wavelengths (see Figs. 7a and 7b). A high QY of 94.4% was reached under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Notably, an up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) phenomenon, consisting in the generation of a shorter wavelength emission when a material is excited at a longer wavelength [69], was observed (Fig. 7b). Specifically, the QY of the CDs under 780 nm excitation reached 6.33%. This UPCL phenomenon demonstrates the potential of these CDs in bioimaging and photocatalysis applications [70,71]. It should be noted that, according to Figs. 7a and 7b, no obvious excitation dependence was observed within the excitation ranges of 340–400 nm and 700–780 nm, respectively. Fig. 7c displays the variation of CD emission light color with increasing excitation wavelength. The CD emission changed from bright blue to red, then turned back to a darker blue. The color change within the excitation range of 450–700 nm exhibited the excitation-dependent fluorescence of the CDs, which is a typical characteristic of carbon core state PL centers [67,72]. The photostability and pH stability of the CDs were also tested (see Figs. S3 and S4 for more details). The CD products exhibited the relatively low photostability (Fig. S5), which was concluded to be a specific property of the molecule state PL centers [48]. In addition, the PL intensity of the CDs remained constant in the pH range of 4–10, only reducing for lower or higher pH levels (Fig. S6). This might be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxylic groups bonded onto the carbon core network, which would influence the excited state relaxation channels and affect the CD PL intensity [64,73].

Fig. 7d illustrates the movement of the PL peak position of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths, revealing their partially excitation-dependent behavior. The excitation dependence of these CDs is not as apparent as that reported for other quantum dots in the previous researches [72,74]. However, the PL peak position still showed a slight blue-shift as the excitation wavelength increased from 305 to 335 nm. In addition, the peak position remained basically stable at about 335–400 nm of the the excitation wavelength and then exhibited a more significant red-shift as the excitation wavelength exceeded 400 nm. It has been reported that the molecule-induced PL generally features a high PL QY and excitation independence [48]. Furthermore, this excitation-independent property can be attributed to the fixed highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gap of a specific molecular fluorophore, which ensures that the maximum emission wavelength of the molecule-state PL will not vary with changing excitation wavelength [30]. Therefore, the reduced excitation dependence of the prepared CDs was attributed to the existence of excitation-independent molecular fluorophores exhibiting the typical single emissive transition of individual molecules [30]. Meanwhile, the carbon cores were accordingly assigned as being responsible for the emission color tunability [67].

The fluorescence lifetime analysis was also performed (Fig. 7e). Although the time-resolved PL decay curves of the prepared CDs were not well fitted with a simple exponential function, they could be successfully reproduced via a biexponential fit. According to the fitting results, the two individual decay lifetimes were 8.927 and 8.940 ns (Fig. 7e and Table S3), respectively, being in a roughly even proportion.
All these facts exhibit the good PL properties of the prepared CDs and can serve as the basis to confirm the existence of the two-state emissive centers discussed in Section 3.2.3.

As a proof-to-concept of the direct application of prepared CDs, their H2O2 detection performances were investigated as well. No variation in the peak position occurred when the concentration of added H2O2 changed according to Fig. S7. Therefore, all the PL intensities of mixture samples were recorded at the emission wavelength of 424 nm. It was noted that the quenching of CD PL here was far from the regular patterns described by the Stern-Volmer equation [37]. Herein, the change in fluorescence strength is denoted as ΔF (ΔF = F0-F), whereas the ΔF/F0 ratio is plotted against the H2O2 concentration to acquire the calibration curve for H2O2 detection via fluorescence analysis.

First, the PL responses at different CD concentrations were measured to determine the optimal dosage of CDs with the most distinctive change in emission intensity. Based on the results from Fig. S8, the dosage of 60 μL was selected as the most optimal for further study. Fig. 8(a) exhibits the standard curve by which the H2O2 concentration is negatively correlated with the F0/F parameter. Particularly, there is a decent linear relationship in the concentration range from around 3 nM (1 ×10−8 wt%) to 3 μM (1 ×10−5 wt%), while the linear fitting becomes invalid at the H2O2 concentrations above 3 μM (1 ×10−5 wt%) (Fig. S9). The detection limit (DL) of this method is as low as 8.6 nM (calculated by the equation DL= 3σ⁄s, where σ represents the standard deviation obtained by repeatedly measuring the blank CD sample for 5 times, and ѕ is the value of the calibration curve slope), which can be attributed to the great stability of the fabricated CDs. Additionally, response time is another crucial factor in H2O2 detection, especially concerning the biological systems [18]. As shown in Fig. S10, an abrupt increase in the value of ΔF/F0 could be detected 1 min after the addition of H2O2. A subsequent slow reduction indicated an obvious PL quenching at the beginning of H2O2 addition and a gradual recovery of fluorescence along the time. A similar trend could be also observed from the corresponding detection images of CD fluorescence response to the addition of H2O2 as shown in Fig. S11. This revealed a unique PL intensity change pattern of the CDs with a strong and fast fluorescent response to H2O2.

The fluorescent response of the CD products to H2O2 does not follow any classic turn-on or turn-off patterns as shown in Fig. S10. Instead, the PL response here displays two distinct stages including a sharp fluorescence decrease and a gradual PL recovering. The possible mechanism is proposed herein. Firstly, H2O2 can react with the free molecular fluorophores (the dominant contributor for the PL of CDs) in the suspension or attached to the carbon core network, resulting in the sharp decrease in PL intensity at the initial stage. Later, once the majority of fluorophores were destroyed, H2O2 would then attack the carbon network structure and may release partial enclosed fluorophore molecules, thus leading to the following slow recovering of PL intensity. However, more abundant and elaborate experimental information is required to thoroughly understand the reaction mechanism between CDs and H2O2 in the future work.

To sum up, the fabricated CDs outperformed over a number of previous materials used in fluorescence analysis (see Fig. 8(b) and Table S4 for more details). Moreover, in order to further prove the suitability of the CDs for in-vivo biological detection, their cytotoxicity was evaluated as well (Text S2). As shown in Fig. S12, the viability of cells could be retained above 99.5% after their incubation in CD solutions within a wide concentration range (expressed by the dosage of CDs in 10 mL of PBS solution). This demonstrated the great biocompatibility and desirable application potential of the proposed CDs for in-vivo biological detection.

In this work, a one-step microwave-assisted treatment route was successfully developed to prepare CDs with a distinguishingly high PL QY of up to 94.4%. The entire process was extremely facile, and the reaction occurred within 3.5 min. The outstanding PL performance could be attributed to the synergistic effects of molecule state and carbon core state fluorescent centers present in CDs. With the aim of providing a fluorescent probe material via a simplified preparation process and the portable instant response, the direct application availability of the fabricated CD products was proven by their decent H2O2 fluorescent detection performances and great biocompatibility. The detection limit for H2O2 could descend to only 8.6 nM, being inferior to most of previously reported fluorescent probe materials. In summary, the microwave-assisted CD synthesis method proposed herein is satisfactorily facile and reliable for the preparation of high QY CDs. It is also a promising strategy to broaden the potential application of these emerging luminescent nanomaterials in the near future, especially as fluorescent probes in biological detection.
Xiaoyu Zhao: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mengyuan Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Zhenghua Zhang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
